text: Another suggestion i have is to make the subset of Intel a bit more regular; in other words, tweak the intel architecture to fix the not-quite orthogonal instruction set. Add memory to memory moves for example (something so wonderful in the Motorola 68000 instruction set), and maybe offer more registers, etc. When Prof. N. Wirth made his Lilith computer, he created a compiler that generated code for the National chip, the Motorola 68k and the intel chip, and his code generator was smallest for the national chip, second best for motorola, and significantly bigger for the intel chip. Interestingly, the market success of the chips was inversely proportional to the easiest chip to program. There is strong evidence that whenever a major choices are made in the computer industry, the one that generates the most billable hours wins. This explains how COBOL beat out far superior languages, and why Java was selected, when it is an awful language.

text: Clojure is a Lisp (a really nice one IMO), and it is definitely competing with Javascript on the server (compiles to JVM) and in the web browser (compiles to Javascript).
Clojure is a Lisp (a really nice one IMO), and it is definitely competing with Javascript on the server (compiles to JVM) and in the web browser (compiles to Javascript).
Clojure seems to be a counter-example earlier on in this thread, showing that parens are not a big deal.
Clojure seems to be a counter-example earlier on in this thread, showing that parens are not a big deal.

As for Bel, it doesn't even have support for numbers, so why are we comparing it to production ready programming languages? I interpret it as an exercise in programming language design, and I believe that useful insights can come out of such an exercise. Programming language design is an important field of study.

Part of what makes Clojure parens "not a big deal" is that Clojure (somehow) fosters projects like Parinfer (<https://shaunlebron.github.io/parinfer/>), which makes writing lisp feel like writing a whitespace language. When I write clojure, I set my parens to a very faint color. It's nice!... and totally not as karmically risky as standing on a mountaintop in the rain wearing copper armour shouting "all gods are bastards".

Part of what makes Clojure parens "not a big deal" is that Clojure (somehow) fosters projects like Parinfer (<https://shaunlebron.github.io/parinfer/>), which makes writing lisp feel like writing a whitespace language. When I write clojure, I set my parens to a very faint color. It's nice!... and totally not as karmically risky as standing on a mountaintop in the rain wearing copper armour shouting "all gods are bastards".

Notice that Parinfer supports non-Clojure Lisps.

It's not the only such tool. Lisp programmers often use tooling to deal with parens.

Is it possible a non-programmer would basically find it extremely hard to break it up? That seems like it's leading to abstractions which by some people's measure is exactly what makes programming hard for non-programmers. Followong that graph is hard but figuring out how a bunch of unconnected more abstract parts fit together would probably be even harder?

<@UJBAJNFLK> Thanks for the clarification. I guess that the definition of a language primitive is: if you implement a primitive in the source language itself, you induce a meta-circular dependency on the language implementation. Non-primitives can be implemented in terms of primitives without creating this kind of circularity. Meta-circular definitions of language primitives are not just a party trick. They are necessary if you want to implement an operating system that runs on top of the bare metal, and if you also want to use that same language & operating system to develop newer versions of itself. The C compiler is written in C. <@ULM3U6275> I agree with your sentiment. Implementing a language in itself, all the way down to the bare metal, is possible, but then you take on the job of supporting all modern hardware, which is a huge commitment.

Nada Amin's "collapsing towers of meta-circular interpreters" might be of interest- <https://www.cs.purdue.edu/homes/rompf/papers/amin-popl18.pdf>

I couldn't write any Lisp code (mostly Scheme and Emacs Lisp these days) without paredit in Emacs, And like <@UC2A2ARPT>, I set parens to a faint color so they don't dominate visually. I am much more efficient writing this than e.g. Python, although I have probably written 10x more Python code than all Lisps taken together.
(Achievement unlocked: I used "10x" to describe myself.)

From a quick look at Bel, it looks a bit like a specification rather than programming language. Except that I don't see the utility of writing specifications at such a low level. So... maybe I'll have another look in a year or two, but I am not holding my breath.

This entertaining presentation of the paper "What about the natural numbers?" makes a case for treating natural numbers more like lists, in a programming context. It even provides a practical motivation for "lazy natural numbers". <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jFk1qpr1ytk>
